synopsis: With Earth collapsing, Bridgers Infinity Fowler and Desmond Weaver lead a group of refugees through an alternate-world escape that goes catastrophically wrong, stranding them on a planet where nothing is familiar and survival demands collective action. The fourth entry in the Bridgers series follows the survivors as primeval dangers and fracturing loyalties test their ability to build any future at all.

sources.title: Bridgers 4: The Mind of Many - Stan C. Smith

synopsis: This anthology assembles Napoleon Bonaparte's letters, speeches, army directives, and St. Helena memoirs into thematic sections spanning war, statecraft, and human nature, edited and arranged by J. Christopher Herold. The selections reveal the range and coherence of Napoleon's thinking across subjects from military strategy to philosophy, offering direct access to his voice rather than secondhand interpretation.

sources.title: Mind of Napoleon: A Selection of His Written and Spoken Words - Amazon

synopsis: Oliver Sacks presents case studies of people who have lost capacities most take for granted—face recognition, spatial perception, reading, or sight itself—and uses these neurological disruptions to illuminate how the brain constructs visual experience. The book draws on Sacks's own diagnosis of eye cancer and lifelong prosopagnosia to connect clinical observation with personal reckoning.

sources.title: The Mind's Eye by Oliver Sacks | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Benoît Mandelbrot applies fractal geometry to financial markets, arguing that conventional models drastically underestimate the frequency and severity of extreme price swings by assuming markets follow a mild, bell-curve distribution. Co-written with Richard Hudson, the book proposes a multifractal framework that better captures the roughness and long-memory behavior observed in real market data.

Victor Haghani and James White examine why wealthy families across history rarely preserved their fortunes across generations, tracing the failure to poor decisions about risk, spending, and investment sizing rather than bad luck. The book presents a framework grounded in expected-utility theory for integrating lifetime saving, spending, and portfolio choices into a coherent financial plan.

Rob Fitzpatrick argues that most customer interviews fail because entrepreneurs ask questions that elicit polite encouragement rather than honest feedback, and he offers a practical method for extracting reliable information about real behavior and unmet needs. The core technique centers on asking about past actions and concrete problems instead of hypothetical preferences or reactions to a pitch.

sources.title: The Mom Test by Rob Fitzpatrick

synopsis: Written under the pseudonym Adam Smith, George Goodman explores the psychology and culture of Wall Street investing through anecdotes, character portraits, and sharp observations about how fear, greed, and crowd behavior drive markets far more than rational analysis. The book treats the stock market as a game with rules shaped as much by human temperament as by financial fundamentals.

sources.title: The Money Game by Adam Smith | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Alok Sama recounts his years as chief dealmaker at SoftBank, navigating mega-deals alongside founder Masayoshi Son while contending with a smear campaign that jeopardized his career and personal life. The memoir traces the euphoria and dysfunction of the late-stage tech investment boom through the perspective of someone inside the deals that defined it.

sources.title: The Money Trap: Lost Illusions Inside the Tech Bubble - Amazon

synopsis: Randy Komisar, drawing on his experience as a Silicon Valley virtual CEO, uses a fictional dialogue with two aspiring entrepreneurs to argue that building a business around deferred passion leads to unfulfilling work and poor decisions. The book contends that integrating personal values and purpose into a venture from the start produces both better companies and more meaningful lives.

sources.title: The Monk and the Riddle | HBR Store

synopsis: Set in 2075, the novel follows a lunar penal colony's revolution against Earth's economic exploitation, orchestrated by a small group of conspirators that includes a self-aware supercomputer named Mike. Heinlein uses the rebellion to examine libertarian political philosophy, the mechanics of revolution, and questions about consciousness and individual freedom.

sources.title: The Moon Is a Harsh Mistress - Wikipedia

synopsis: Howard Marks distills his investment philosophy into principles covering second-level thinking, risk assessment, market cycles, and the psychology of patient opportunism, with commentary added by investors including Joel Greenblatt and Seth Klarman. The annotated edition extends the original text by embedding practical counterpoints and illustrative examples alongside Marks's core arguments.

sources.title: The Most Important Thing Illuminated | Columbia Scholarship Online

synopsis: Five survivors of a Civil War balloon crash land on an uncharted island and apply engineering knowledge and ingenuity to build a self-sufficient settlement from near nothing, while a mysterious benefactor intervenes at critical moments. The novel reveals that benefactor to be Captain Nemo, connecting it to Verne's earlier work and closing his submarine saga.

sources.title: The Mysterious Island | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Frederick Brooks draws on his experience managing the IBM OS/360 project to argue that adding engineers to a late software project makes it later, and that large programming efforts require conceptual integrity that only a small, coordinated team can achieve. The essays address scheduling, communication overhead, and the fundamental difficulty of building complex software systems.

Daron Acemoglu and James Robinson argue that liberty persists only in a narrow corridor where state power and societal resistance remain in rough balance, and that tipping too far toward either despotism or anarchy destroys it. The book surveys historical cases across continents to show how this contested equilibrium forms, holds, and collapses.

Balaji Srinivasan proposes that online communities can evolve into sovereign entities—network states—by building shared values, crowdfunding physical territory, and eventually gaining diplomatic recognition, enabled by blockchain technology and remote coordination. The book outlines a framework for how this process could unfold as an alternative to reforming or seceding from existing nation-states.

sources.title: The Network State by Balaji Srinivasan | CoinMarketCap Academy

synopsis: Daniel Yergin maps how the shale revolution transformed the United States into the world's largest energy producer, how China's rise and Russia's leverage are reshaping geopolitical alignments, and how the global push toward low-carbon energy is accelerating a second energy transition. The book traces how these forces are redrawing the strategic map that governs international relations.

sources.title: The New Map by Daniel Yergin | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Hard science fiction novel following NASA analyst Melody Adams, who identifies an anomalous object near Pluto that she suspects is an extrasolar spacecraft, only to have her career derailed before a second approach toward Saturn forces a mission to investigate. The story centers on first-contact communication with an intelligence whose mode of existence makes finding common ground nearly impossible.

sources.title: Book Review: The Object by Joshua T. Calvert | New Space Economy

synopsis: Amar Bhide draws on a decade of research to show that most new businesses begin with improvised, low-capital opportunities pursued without detailed plans or breakthrough technologies, and that this humble starting point is not a weakness but a feature of how entrepreneurial markets function. The book provides an empirical framework for understanding how new ventures originate, evolve, and contribute to economic dynamism.

sources.title: The Origin and Evolution of New Businesses | Oxford University Press

synopsis: Eric Beinhocker surveys complexity economics and evolutionary theory to argue that wealth creation follows the logic of biological evolution—differentiate, select, amplify—rather than the equilibrium models of traditional economics. The book contends that treating the economy as a complex adaptive system, akin to an ecosystem or the brain, better explains how markets generate novelty, growth, and instability.

sources.title: The Origin of Wealth | Publishers Weekly
